content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 24, 2008


Elmo Garcia
831 East D Street
Oakdale, CA 95361

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-08-081

Dear Mr. Garcia:

       This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ You have not provided the necessary
information to provide you with formal advice, so we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).² During our telephone conversation
on June 16, 2008, you asked an additional question we will address in this letter.

QUESTIONS

1. Is the Oak Valley Hospital District (OVHD) required to post an open meeting
   agenda in the local newspaper?

2. You intend to organize local citizens to oppose a proposed bond measure for the
   hospital district. Are you required to obtain a committee identification number if
   you collect contributions?

3. During the telephone conversation referenced above, you asked whether there
   would be any reporting obligations if you and your wife did not raise money from
   other persons, or coordinate any activities with others, but made payments to
   communicate with the voters your opposition to the bond measure?

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; Title 2 Cal. Code of Regs., Section 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-08-081
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

1. We have no jurisdiction over and do not provide advice in regards to the statutes
governing public meetings. However, you may call the state Attorney General's
advice line for questions concerning the Brown Act at (800) 952-5225.
2. Once $1,000 or more in a calendar year is raised to oppose the qualification or
passage of a ballot measure, including a hospital bond measure, a recipient
committee has been qualified. A Statement of Organization (Form 410) must
then be filed with the Secretary of State's office within 10 days, and a copy
provided to the county filing officer. (In Stanislaus County, the filing officer is
the County Clerk-Recorder.) The Secretary of State will then assign an
identification number for the committee.
3. As we discussed during our telephone conversation, if you do not raise money or
coordinate your activities with other persons, but make independent expenditures
from personal funds aggregating $1,000 or more in a calendar year to
communicate to the voters your opposition to the bond measure, you would have
reporting obligations as an independent expenditure committee. However, if you
spend less than $1,000 in personal funds, you would have no reporting obligations
under the Act.

FACTS

You state that the Oak Valley Hospital District, located in Oakdale, California, is
planning to place a $27,000,000 bond measure on the ballot for voter approval in January
2009.³ You intend to organize a group of residents to oppose the bond measure. Your
efforts will include raising funds to support your political activities.

ANALYSIS

Section 82013(a) defines a committee as "any person or combination of persons
who directly or indirectly . . . receives contributions totaling one thousand ($1,000) or
more in a calendar year." A contribution is defined in Section 82015, in part, as a

". . . payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan
by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a
payment except to the extent that full and adequate
consideration is received, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes."

³ According to the Stanislaus County Clerk-Recorder's office, the mail-in ballots for the general
obligation bond measure are due to the county's election office August 26, 2008. Your letter indicated the
election would be in January 2009. Nevertheless, in our referenced telephone conversation, you agreed that
the election about which your questions address will be held in August 2008.

File No. I-08-081
Page No. 3

Regulation 18215 defines a "payment for political purposes" as a payment

". . . [f]or the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or the
qualification or passage of any measure; . . ."

Section 84101 requires "[a] committee that is a committee by virtue of
subdivision (a) of Section 82013," to file a statement of organization with the Secretary
of State within 10 days after it has qualified as a committee. Thus, once you have raised
$1,000 for the purpose of opposing the passage of the bond measure, you must file within
10 days a statement of Organization (Form 410) with the Secretary of State. (You can
find an interactive version of Form 410 our website at www.fppc.ca.gov. The
instructions for Form 410 provide the address for the Secretary of State's office.) You
must also file a copy of the Form 410 with the Stanislaus County Clerk-Recorder.
(Section 84101.) Upon receipt of the Form 410, the Secretary of State will assign a
number to the new committee. (Section 84101.)

In addition, Section 82013, subdivision (b) defines a committee as:

". . . any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly . . . Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; . ."

An independent expenditure is defined in Section 82031 as:

". . . an expenditure made by any person in connection with
a communication which expressly advocates the
election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified
measure, or taken as a whole and in context,
unambiguously urges a particular result in an election but
which is not made to or at the behest of the affected
candidate or committee."

Regulation 18225.7 defines "made at the behest of" as:

". . . made under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, at
the request or suggestion of, or with the express, prior
consent of. Such arrangement must occur prior to the
making of a communication described in Government Code
section 82031."

File No. I-08-081
Page No. 4

Thus, using only personal funds, and on your own (or with your spouse) and without
communicating or coordinating your activity with any other person, if you make
payments to communicate with the voters your opposition to the ballot measure, and
these payments total $1,000 or more in a calendar year, you will be considered an
independent expenditure committee. Independent expenditure committees must report
their activities. However, if your expenditures are under $1,000 in a calendar year, you
will not qualify as a committee and, therefore, will have no reporting obligations under
the Act.

Based on your proposed activity as stated in your letter, we recommend that you
refer to Campaign Disclosure Manual 3, Information for Committees Primarily Formed
to Support or Oppose a Ballot Measure. This manual is available on the Commission's
website. We have included a copy for your convenience with this letter. If you choose to
spend personal funds totaling $1,000 or more, please contact the Commission for advice
regarding your filing obligations as an independent expenditure committee.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

KSM:jgl

Enclosure
